summary: Paul Graham argues that your best work depends on what your mind naturally drifts to during “ambient” moments, like in the shower. Because that top idea gets the most unconscious processing, letting the wrong thing dominate it—especially fundraising or disputes—starves more important work of attention. The practical takeaway is to shape your situations so the most urgent problems are the ones you want your mind to keep returning to.
key_points: Ambient, unforced thinking is crucial for solving hard problems, and it mainly benefits whatever your mind treats as the top idea.
Raising money and disputes are attention sinks that crowd out better work, slowing startups and individuals alike.
You can’t directly control drifting thoughts, but you can control situations so the right problems become top-of-mind.
Ignoring injuries and disputes is not just virtuous but protective of your attention.
A simple diagnostic is to notice what your thoughts return to in the shower; if it’s wrong, change something.
money_quotes: It’s hard to do a really good job on anything you don’t think about in the shower.
I think most people have one top idea in their mind at any given time.
Which means it’s a disaster to let the wrong idea become the top one in your mind.
Money matters are particularly likely to become the top idea in your mind.
Someone who does you an injury hurts you twice: first by the injury itself, and second by taking up your time afterward thinking about it.
key_insight: Shape your commitments so your mind’s default drift serves the work you value most, because whatever sits at the top of your thoughts will quietly determine your progress.
